texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There has been a serious concern about global warming caused by the emission of carbon dioxide (CO2) from various sources, and a need has arisen to deal with global warming with international collaboration. The basic principles for preventing that problem are to constrain consumption of fossil fuels and get away from dependence on them. However it is difficult to convert present energy sources to renewable energies in a short period. Therefore, focus has been placed on CCS (carbon capture and storage) technology, and trial operations are carried out in several places. Not only employing CCS technology but also reducing its cost is necessary for applying CCS to practical use as one of the measures for preventing global warming. The principal costs of CCS consist of CO2 capture, transport and injection, and there is a demand to decrease the cost of capture because it occupies a large portion of the total cost of the CCS process.
texts.text: There has been a serious concern about global warming caused by the emission of carbon dioxide (CO2) from various sources, and a need has arisen to deal with global warming with international collaboration. The basic principles for preventing that problem are to constrain consumption of fossil fuels and get away from dependence on them. However it is difficult to convert present energy sources to renewable energies in a short period. Therefore, focus has been placed on CCS (carbon capture and storage) technology, and trial operations are carried out in several places. Not only employing CCS technology but also reducing its cost is necessary for applying CCS to practical use as one of the measures for preventing global warming. The principal costs of CCS consist of CO2 capture, transport and injection, and there is a demand to decrease the cost of capture because it occupies a large portion of the total cost of the CCS process.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: a b s t r a c t
texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A novel process for carbon dioxide (CO2) separation, which was named a membrane flash process, was developed to realize an energy-saving technology and to substitute it for a conventional regenerator. The electric energy for CO2 recovery in a membrane flash process using aluminum oxide and diethanolamine was lower than the thermal energy of the conventional chemical absorption process. Flashing at elevated temperature by the low temperature energy significantly reduced the electric energy and required much less membrane area. This process has potentiality of low cost capture of CO2 temperature energy, which is not available for other purposes, can be utilized to elevate flashing temperature.
texts.text: A novel process for carbon dioxide (CO2) separation, which was named a membrane flash process, was developed to realize an energy-saving technology and to substitute it for a conventional regenerator. The electric energy for CO2 recovery in a membrane flash process using aluminum oxide and diethanolamine was lower than the thermal energy of the conventional chemical absorption process. Flashing at elevated temperature by the low temperature energy significantly reduced the electric energy and required much less membrane area. This process has potentiality of low cost capture of CO2 temperature energy, which is not available for other purposes, can be utilized to elevate flashing temperature.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: # 2008 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
texts.text: # 2008 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Research Institute of Innovative Technology for the Earth (RITE) has been developing a facilitated transport membrane for CO2 separation which has much higher selectivity than conventional polymeric membranes (Okabe et al., 2007), because the energy for CO2 recovery by a membrane depends on the selectivity. Since the facilitated transport membrane has low stability in general, many approaches have been carried out such as gelation, polymerization of the top layer, sandwich configuration, solidification and new liquid membrane configuration etc. (Kemperman et al., 1996).
texts.text: The Research Institute of Innovative Technology for the Earth (RITE) has been developing a facilitated transport membrane for CO2 separation which has much higher selectivity than conventional polymeric membranes (Okabe et al., 2007), because the energy for CO2 recovery by a membrane depends on the selectivity. Since the facilitated transport membrane has low stability in general, many approaches have been carried out such as gelation, polymerization of the top layer, sandwich configuration, solidification and new liquid membrane configuration etc. (Kemperman et al., 1996).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Anovel membrane process was also proposed to overcome the instability problems of a facilitated transport membrane. Teramoto et al. (2002) reported that the liquid membrane accompanied by permeation of carrier solution worked well for CO2 separation from a carbon dioxide and methane (CO2/ CH4) mixed gas stream in a flat membrane. About ten times higher permeance of CO2 than that of usual facilitated

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Wehavebeen studying the modification and improvement of this process, which is called a membrane flash process in this paper, to apply it for practical use in the field of CO2 separation and recovery. The principle of this process is illustrated in Fig. 1. The rich solution that absorbed CO2 in the absorber is supplied to the inside of hollow fibers and it is forced to permeate the membrane. The rich solution releases CO2 during permeating through the membrane to the outside of hollow fibers in which the pressure is reduced. The advantages of this process are that high CO2 concentration and low energy consumption are realized (Okabe et al., 2006), the membrane is stable, and conventional microporous

The principle of this process is illustrated in Fig. 1. The rich solution that absorbed CO2 in the absorber is supplied to the inside of hollow fibers and it is forced to permeate the membrane. The rich solution releases CO2 during permeating through the membrane to the outside of hollow fibers in which the pressure is reduced. The advantages of this process are that high CO2 concentration and low energy consumption are realized (Okabe et al., 2006), the membrane is stable, and conventional microporous

texts.orig: membranes can be used. In the present study, the membrane flash process was developed to realize energy-saving technology utilizing low temperature energy, and to substitute it for a conventional regenerator.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Methodology
texts.text: 2. Methodology

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Experimental
texts.text: 2.1. Experimental

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We fabricated hollow fiber membrane modules which were made of polysulfone (PS; Amersham Biosciences Corp.), polyethersulfone (PES; Daicel Chemical Industries, Ltd.), polyethylene (PE; Asahi Kasei Chemicals Corp.), polyvinylidenefluoride (PVdF; Asahi Kasei Chemicals Corp.), polytetrafluoroethylene(PTFE;SumitomoElectricFinePolymer, Inc.), and aluminum oxide (Al2O3; NGK Insulators, Ltd.). The specifications of hollow fibers and membrane modules are listed in Table 1, and the experimental conditions are shown in Table 2. These hollow fibers are used for ultrafiltration or microfiltration in the industry. Aqueous diethanolamine (DEA) solution of 2 mol/l was examined as the CO2 absorbent solution in this process. Carbon dioxide and nitrogen mixed gas (CO2/N2, CO2 mole fraction was 0.15) with saturated water vapour was used as a simulated flue gas. Absorption and flashing were performed at several temperatures shown in Table 2.
texts.text: We fabricated hollow fiber membrane modules which were made of polysulfone (PS; Amersham Biosciences Corp.), polyethersulfone (PES; Daicel Chemical Industries, Ltd.), polyethylene (PE; Asahi Kasei Chemicals Corp.), polyvinylidenefluoride (PVdF; Asahi Kasei Chemicals Corp.), polytetrafluoroethylene(PTFE;SumitomoElectricFinePolymer, Inc.), and aluminum oxide (Al2O3; NGK Insulators, Ltd.). The specifications of hollow fibers and membrane modules are listed in Table 1, and the experimental conditions are shown in Table 2. These hollow fibers are used for ultrafiltration or microfiltration in the industry. Aqueous diethanolamine (DEA) solution of 2 mol/l was examined as the CO2 absorbent solution in this process. Carbon dioxide and nitrogen mixed gas (CO2/N2, CO2 mole fraction was 0.15) with saturated water vapour was used as a simulated flue gas. Absorption and flashing were performed at several temperatures shown in Table 2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theschematic diagram of the experimental setup is shown in Fig. 2. The simulated flue gas was synthesized by mixing each gas, which controlled its flow rate, by a mass flow
texts.text: Theschematic diagram of the experimental setup is shown in Fig. 2. The simulated flue gas was synthesized by mixing each gas, which controlled its flow rate, by a mass flow

texts.orig: i n t e r n a t i o n a l j o u r n a l of greenhouse gas control 2 (2008) 485-491

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 2 - Schematic diagram of experimental setup.
texts.text: Fig. 2 - Schematic diagram of experimental setup.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: controller. The simulated flue gas was supplied to a bubble column-type absorber at a pressure slightly higher than the atmospheric pressure. The absorbent solution was also supplied to the absorber by a liquid circulation pump and it became rich solution. The rich solution was fed to the inner side of the hollow fibers in the module by another circulation pump.Theouterside of the hollow fibers was evacuated at the low pressure P s . P s included the CO2 partial pressure P CO2 and a water vapor pressure P W. P CO2 was controlled at about 5 kPa in every experiment and P W was assumed to be the saturated water vapor pressure at the operating temperature. Therefore, the total pressure P s was different at each temperature. Most of the rich solution permeated and flashed through the membranes, then the rich solution released CO2 to become lean solution. A small amount of rich solution flowed through the inside of the hollow fibers and overflowed at the top of the module, then it returned to a liquid reservoir. The lean solution and released gas returned to the reservoir and the solution was recycled to the feed side. The released gas was passed through a condenser and it was discharged from a vacuum pump. Its flow rate was measured by a digital soapfilm flow meter and the gas composition was determined by a gas chromatograph with two kinds of columns (Yanaco, GC2800), which were Porapak Q for CO2 and N2 analysis, and molecular sieve 5A for N2 and O2 analysis to compensate for air leakage. The performances and energies were calculated from these data.
texts.text: controller. The simulated flue gas was supplied to a bubble column-type absorber at a pressure slightly higher than the atmospheric pressure. The absorbent solution was also supplied to the absorber by a liquid circulation pump and it became rich solution. The rich solution was fed to the inner side of the hollow fibers in the module by another circulation pump.Theouterside of the hollow fibers was evacuated at the low pressure P s . P s included the CO2 partial pressure P CO2 and a water vapor pressure P W. P CO2 was controlled at about 5 kPa in every experiment and P W was assumed to be the saturated water vapor pressure at the operating temperature. Therefore, the total pressure P s was different at each temperature. Most of the rich solution permeated and flashed through the membranes, then the rich solution released CO2 to become lean solution. A small amount of rich solution flowed through the inside of the hollow fibers and overflowed at the top of the module, then it returned to a liquid reservoir. The lean solution and released gas returned to the reservoir and the solution was recycled to the feed side. The released gas was passed through a condenser and it was discharged from a vacuum pump. Its flow rate was measured by a digital soapfilm flow meter and the gas composition was determined by a gas chromatograph with two kinds of columns (Yanaco, GC2800), which were Porapak Q for CO2 and N2 analysis, and molecular sieve 5A for N2 and O2 analysis to compensate for air leakage. The performances and energies were calculated from these data.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2. Energy estimation
texts.text: 2.2. Energy estimation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The energy for CO2 recovery in this process can be regarded as the electric energy of major equipment such as a blower, a vacuum pump and liquid pumps for absorbent solution, hot water and coolant. It does not include the thermal energy, because the available heat such as high temperature steam is not consumed for regeneration of the rich solution. Energy estimations were carried out under the condition that the CO2 recovery ratio was 80%.
texts.text: The energy for CO2 recovery in this process can be regarded as the electric energy of major equipment such as a blower, a vacuum pump and liquid pumps for absorbent solution, hot water and coolant. It does not include the thermal energy, because the available heat such as high temperature steam is not consumed for regeneration of the rich solution. Energy estimations were carried out under the condition that the CO2 recovery ratio was 80%.

The flow rate of overflowing liquid circulation is 10% of the total flow rate. h ab, h mf and h of are 17 m, 12 m and 10 m, respectively. h ex varies between 6 m to 9 m depending on the pressure of the membrane module.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Results and discussion
texts.text: 3. Results and discussion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The overall mass transfer coefficients ( K o) vs. liquid flow rates ( V L) are summarized in Fig. 3. Both absorbing and flashing temperatures were 30 8 C. The whole data of overall mass transfer coefficients were regarded as proportional to liquid flow rates V L in each material. The module performance is represented by the proportional constant K o/ V L that meansthe amount of released CO2 in a unit liquid flow rate. Therefore, a larger proportional constant means a higher release property. From the results of Fig. 3, we can find that Al2O3 and PE had larger ratios of K o/ V L and they were excellent materials for the membrane flash process. Polymer membranes, except PE, had lower performances.
texts.text: The overall mass transfer coefficients ( K o) vs. liquid flow rates ( V L) are summarized in Fig. 3. Both absorbing and flashing temperatures were 30 8 C. The whole data of overall mass transfer coefficients were regarded as proportional to liquid flow rates V L in each material. The module performance is represented by the proportional constant K o/ V L that meansthe amount of released CO2 in a unit liquid flow rate. Therefore, a larger proportional constant means a higher release property. From the results of Fig. 3, we can find that Al2O3 and PE had larger ratios of K o/ V L and they were excellent materials for the membrane flash process. Polymer membranes, except PE, had lower performances.

texts.orig: Al2O3 module that had the highest liquid flow rate. The electric energies were calculated from the experimental data for a blower, liquid pumps and a vacuum pump. From these results, it was found that both the electric energy and the required membrane area were lowest in the Al2O3 module. The electric energy for the Al2O3 module was about 0.2 kWh kg-CO2 � 1 or lower.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The energy items for each equipment are shown in Fig. 5. Electric energy for membrane flash is shown on the left, and thermal energy for chemical absorption is shown on the right. They can be converted to each other on the assumption that the efficiency of electric power generation is 40%. The electric energy of a membrane flash process was about half of the thermal energy of a chemical absorption process which was calculated by The Kansai Electric Power Company. They carried out the pilot plant test using KS-1 for CO2 absorbent solution whose gas throughput was 600 m 3 h � 1 and reported that the energy for regeneration was 3.9 GJ ton-CO2 � 1 (Mimura et al., 2001).
texts.text: The energy items for each equipment are shown in Fig. 5. Electric energy for membrane flash is shown on the left, and thermal energy for chemical absorption is shown on the right. They can be converted to each other on the assumption that the efficiency of electric power generation is 40%. The electric energy of a membrane flash process was about half of the thermal energy of a chemical absorption process which was calculated by The Kansai Electric Power Company. They carried out the pilot plant test using KS-1 for CO2 absorbent solution whose gas throughput was 600 m 3 h � 1 and reported that the energy for regeneration was 3.9 GJ ton-CO2 � 1 (Mimura et al., 2001).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, the membrane flash process had one significant problem in that only a small amount of absorbent solution worked well in CO2 desorption, so that a large amount of solution was necessary, and thus the energy of a liquid circulation pump became higher. This weak point causes an increase in the cost, so we are studying to solve this problem utilizing low temperature energy or waste heat which is not available for other uses. In the case of applying this process for the steel industry, the heat of exhaust gas from furnaces (150250 8 C), the heat of coke oven gas (600-800 8 C) or the heat of slag from a blast furnace and a converter (1500-1600 8 C) will be available for elevating the flash temperature. In the case of applying it for the oil refinery process, the heat of products will be utilized for this purpose. Most of these heats are not recovered now because of low temperature or technical difficulty. The experimental studies of the membrane flash process at elevated temperature were carried out to confirm its usefulness and to obtain basic data for the case study and quantitative estimation of energy.
texts.text: However, the membrane flash process had one significant problem in that only a small amount of absorbent solution worked well in CO2 desorption, so that a large amount of solution was necessary, and thus the energy of a liquid circulation pump became higher. This weak point causes an increase in the cost, so we are studying to solve this problem utilizing low temperature energy or waste heat which is not available for other uses. In the case of applying this process for the steel industry, the heat of exhaust gas from furnaces (150250 8 C), the heat of coke oven gas (600-800 8 C) or the heat of slag from a blast furnace and a converter (1500-1600 8 C) will be available for elevating the flash temperature. In the case of applying it for the oil refinery process, the heat of products will be utilized for this purpose. Most of these heats are not recovered now because of low temperature or technical difficulty. The experimental studies of the membrane flash process at elevated temperature were carried out to confirm its usefulness and to obtain basic data for the case study and quantitative estimation of energy.

texts.orig: The rich solution passes through a pre-heat-exchanger to recover thermal energy from the lean solution. After that it is heated by hot water to the appointed temperature and then fed to the module. The lean solution through the pre-heatexchanger is cooled by sea water, and returns to the absorber.

After that it is heated by hot water to the appointed temperature and then fed to the module. The lean solution through the pre-heatexchanger is cooled by sea water, and returns to the absorber.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Experimental results at elevated temperature are shown in Fig. 7 in the same form as in Fig. 3. These experiments were carried out at 40 8 C in absorbing and 40 8 C to 70 8 C in flashing. The aluminum oxide that had the highest performance was used for the membrane material. Measurements at 50 8 C to 70 8 C were done at several flashing pressures to estimate its effect on the performance. Gray circle plots in Fig. 7 are the results at 30 8 C shown in the previous Fig. 3. With increasing flashing temperature, the higher the performance was to a great extent. The dependence of flashing pressure on the performance is described using Fig. 11 below.

Measurements at 50 8 C to 70 8 C were done at several flashing pressures to estimate its effect on the performance. Gray circle plots in Fig. 7 are the results at 30 8 C shown in the previous Fig. 3. With increasing flashing temperature, the higher the performance was to a great extent. The dependence of flashing pressure on the performance is described using Fig. 11 below.

texts.orig: A release ratio R R was defined as the ratio of CO2 loading (mol-CO2/mol-DEA) as shown in equation (3) below.
texts.text: A release ratio R R was defined as the ratio of CO2 loading (mol-CO2/mol-DEA) as shown in equation (3) below.

texts.label: formula

texts.orig: Here, L eq.a is the CO2 loading equilibrated with the CO2 partial pressure at an absorber. L eq.f is the loading equilibrated with the CO2 partial pressure at a membrane module, with the saturated water vapor pressure subtracted from the total pressure. L eq.a and L eq.f were calculated from the equilibrium data (Lee et al., 1972; Lal et al., 1980). L L is the loading of a lean solution. L eq.a � L eq.f means the loading of releasable CO2 and L eq.a � L L means that of released CO2. Therefore, the ratio R R becomes the index of CO2 release ability.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Release ratios at each flashing temperature are shown in Fig. 8. The release ratio was about 10% or lower at 40 8 Cor50 8 C as was mentioned in Fig. 5. The release ratio increased with the flashing temperature up to about 22%, however it was still low. This result means that the membrane flash process has a potentiality of increasing its performance through further developments such as material research or process design.
texts.text: Release ratios at each flashing temperature are shown in Fig. 8. The release ratio was about 10% or lower at 40 8 Cor50 8 C as was mentioned in Fig. 5. The release ratio increased with the flashing temperature up to about 22%, however it was still low. This result means that the membrane flash process has a potentiality of increasing its performance through further developments such as material research or process design.

Both energies and required membrane areas decreased to about half in energy and to one tenth in area with increasing flashing temperature. From these results, it was found that raising temperature in

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Energy items for equipment at each temperature are shown in Fig. 10 in the same form as Fig. 5. The energies of liquid circulation pumps for hot water to heat the rich solution and cold water to cool the lean solution were added in the elevated temperature operations. The electric energy of circulation pumps for absorbent solution decreased drastically with increasing flashing temperature, because the amount of circulating solution substantially decreased. Consequently, it decreased the total energy, even though the excess energies for hot water and cold water pumps were added.Thisprocessneedslowtemperaturethermalenergyto raise the temperature of rich solution, so the capital cost is thought to be high. However, the quality of this energy is low, so that the cost of this low temperature energy is considered to be cheep.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: pressure P CO2 and the saturated water vapor pressure P W*. The flashing pressure has great influences on several factors of the membrane flash performance. When the flashing pressure increases at the same temperature, then first, (a) flow rate of absorbent solution V L decreases, however it does not result in the decrease of energy, if the overall mass transfer coefficients K o is in proportion to V L as shown in Fig. 3. Second, (b) the P CO2 increases ( P W* is constant) and results in the decrease of recovered CO2 and the increase of energy. Third, (c) the energy of a vacuum pump decreases. From the results of Fig. 11, it is thought that the effect of (b) is predominant at 50 8 C, the effects of (b) and (c) influence the equal degree at 60 8 Candthe effect of (c) is superior at 70 8 C. The electric energy has a minimum value at either flashing pressure, so that the optimum flashing pressure minimizes the energy consumption.
texts.text: pressure P CO2 and the saturated water vapor pressure P W*. The flashing pressure has great influences on several factors of the membrane flash performance. When the flashing pressure increases at the same temperature, then first, (a) flow rate of absorbent solution V L decreases, however it does not result in the decrease of energy, if the overall mass transfer coefficients K o is in proportion to V L as shown in Fig. 3. Second, (b) the P CO2 increases ( P W* is constant) and results in the decrease of recovered CO2 and the increase of energy. Third, (c) the energy of a vacuum pump decreases. From the results of Fig. 11, it is thought that the effect of (b) is predominant at 50 8 C, the effects of (b) and (c) influence the equal degree at 60 8 Candthe effect of (c) is superior at 70 8 C. The electric energy has a minimum value at either flashing pressure, so that the optimum flashing pressure minimizes the energy consumption.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusion
texts.text: 4. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A novel process for carbon dioxide separation which was named a membrane flash process was developed to realize an energy-saving technology and to substitute it for a conventional regenerator. Furthermore, utilizing low temperature energy for the membrane flash process was studied to cut down energy consumption. As a result, the following conclusions were obtained:
texts.text: A novel process for carbon dioxide separation which was named a membrane flash process was developed to realize an energy-saving technology and to substitute it for a conventional regenerator. Furthermore, utilizing low temperature energy for the membrane flash process was studied to cut down energy consumption. As a result, the following conclusions were obtained:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Aluminum oxide was the most suitable material for the membrane flash process in commercial membranes.
texts.text: Aluminum oxide was the most suitable material for the membrane flash process in commercial membranes.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. The electric energy for CO2 recovery in a membrane flash process was lower than the thermal energy of the chemical absorption process. Flashing at elevated temperature by low temperature energy significantly reduced the electric energy and required much less membrane area.
texts.text: The electric energy for CO2 recovery in a membrane flash process was lower than the thermal energy of the chemical absorption process. Flashing at elevated temperature by low temperature energy significantly reduced the electric energy and required much less membrane area.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. This process has potentiality to realize low cost technology for CO2 capture when low temperature energy which is not available for other purposes can be utilized to elevate flashing temperature.
texts.text: This process has potentiality to realize low cost technology for CO2 capture when low temperature energy which is not available for other purposes can be utilized to elevate flashing temperature.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. The case study of the flashing process at elevated temperature and the test of long term stability have to be performed.
texts.text: The case study of the flashing process at elevated temperature and the test of long term stability have to be performed.
